summary: The East Village Everything application has one critical and multiple high-severity vulnerabilities that block deployment. The most severe issue is stored Cross-Site Scripting on the public homepage: the EJS template renders the 'specials' and 'notes' fields with the unescaped <%- operator, and the model layer stores user-supplied newlines as raw HTML without sanitization, allowing any stored script tag to execute in every visitor's browser. Compounding this, the application has no CSRF protection on any POST endpoint, meaning an attacker can trigger admin state-changes (create, update, delete places and tags, logout) by tricking an authenticated admin into loading a malicious page. The admin JSON API additionally accepts unsanitized req.body directly in create and update calls (mass assignment). Infrastructure concerns include a wildcard CORS configuration that permits credentialed cross-origin requests from any origin, a session secret with a hardcoded fallback, a Redis instance with no authentication, session cookies missing the SameSite attribute, and missing HSTS and Content-Security-Policy headers on the public vhost. A hardcoded default admin password ('changeme123') is documented in version-controlled migration source code. None of the public-facing SQL queries use string interpolation — all use parameterized statements — so SQL injection is not a finding. EJS escaping (<%=) is used correctly in all admin views and for most fields in the public view; only specials and notes are unescaped by design, which is the root of the XSS finding.

owasp_findings.title: Stored XSS via unescaped specials and notes fields on public homepage
owasp_findings.description:
The EJS template renders place.specials (line 87) and place.notes (line 91) using the unescaped output operator '<%-'. The model layer (src/models/place.ts:37-40) converts newlines to '
' tags but does not strip or encode other HTML. An admin who saves '' in either field will have that payload executed in the browser of every public visitor who loads the homepage.
owasp_findings.remediation:
Either (1) sanitize specials and notes at write time using a library such as DOMPurify (server-side via jsdom) or sanitize-html, restricting allowed tags to '
' only; or (2) store the fields as plain text and render them safely using '<%=' with CSS white-space:pre-wrap instead of converting newlines to HTML. Do not use '<%- ' for any field that originates from user input unless it has been explicitly sanitized to a safe HTML allowlist.

owasp_findings.title: No CSRF protection on any state-changing POST route
owasp_findings.description: The application has no CSRF token middleware installed or referenced (csurf, csrf-csrf, or equivalent). Every POST route — login, logout, create/update/delete for places and tags — can be triggered cross-origin from any page the admin visits. The admin AJAX endpoints in the same file (e.g., PATCH /admin/api/tags/:id at line 441) are protected by the session cookie but no CSRF token, so a page on any origin can issue a fetch() with credentials and mutate data.
owasp_findings.remediation: Install the 'csrf-csrf' or 'csurf' package (the latter is deprecated; prefer csrf-csrf). Generate a per-session token, inject it into every admin form as a hidden field and into every EJS template, and validate it on every non-GET route. For the AJAX PATCH endpoint at /admin/api/tags/:id, also validate the token from the request header (e.g., X-CSRF-Token).

owasp_findings.title: req.body passed directly to PlaceModel.create and PlaceModel.update in admin JSON API
owasp_findings.description: The admin JSON API routes POST /admin/api/places (line 407) and PUT /admin/api/places/:id (line 412) pass req.body directly to PlaceModel.create() and PlaceModel.update() without any field whitelist or schema validation. An attacker who can reach these endpoints (authenticated session or via CSRF) can supply arbitrary fields in the JSON body.
owasp_findings.remediation: Extract only the fields that are valid inputs (name, address, phone, url, specials, categories, notes, tags) from req.body before passing to the model, mirroring the explicit destructuring already applied in the form-based POST /admin/places route at line 124. Apply the same fix to PATCH /admin/api/tags/:id at line 442.

owasp_findings.title: Unvalidated URL field stored and rendered as href on public page and admin page
owasp_findings.description: place.url is stored as supplied and rendered directly as the href of an anchor tag in src/views/public/index.ejs line 75 and src/views/admin/places/index.ejs lines 161 and 195. No protocol validation is enforced in the route handler or model. A value of 'javascript:alert(document.cookie)' or 'data:text/html,<script>...' will execute JavaScript when a user clicks the link. The HTML attribute itself is EJS-escaped (<%=) so injection into the attribute context is blocked, but the javascript: protocol bypass is independent of escaping.
owasp_findings.remediation: In PlaceModel.create() and PlaceModel.update() (src/models/place.ts), validate that the url field begins with 'https://' or 'http://' before storing. Reject or strip values with any other protocol. Alternatively, add a server-side URL parse check using the URL constructor and verify that url.protocol is 'https:' or 'http:'.

owasp_findings.title: Default admin credentials hardcoded and documented in version-controlled migration
owasp_findings.description: The seed migration commits the bcrypt hash of the password 'changeme123' and documents the plaintext password in a code comment at line 7. This migration runs automatically on deployment. If the admin password is not changed after first deployment, the account is trivially compromised by anyone who reads the repository. Because the hash is also committed, offline cracking is made easier.
owasp_findings.remediation: Remove the plaintext password from the comment immediately. Do not seed a known default password into production. Instead, seed the admin account with a randomly generated password that is written to a deployment-time secret (e.g., a GitHub Actions secret or AWS Secrets Manager), logged once, and then only changeable through a password-reset flow. Alternatively, remove the seed migration entirely and add a first-run CLI script that generates and stores a secure random password.

owasp_findings.title: CORS configured with origin: true and credentials: true, allowing all origins
owasp_findings.description: The CORS middleware is configured with 'origin: true' (which reflects the request's Origin header back as the allowed origin) combined with 'credentials: true'. This effectively grants every origin permission to make credentialed cross-origin requests, including to the admin API endpoints at /admin/api/*. This undermines the session-cookie-based authentication boundary.
owasp_findings.remediation: Replace 'origin: true' with an explicit allowlist of origins: the public site domain for the public API, and no cross-origin access for admin routes. For the public API (/api/*) which is intentionally public, CORS may be open but credentials should be set to false. Admin routes should not be accessible from cross-origin at all — consider removing CORS headers from the admin router entirely or setting 'origin: false'.

owasp_findings.title: Session secret has a hardcoded fallback value
owasp_findings.description: The session configuration uses 'process.env.SESSION_SECRET || "dev-secret-change-in-production"'. If SESSION_SECRET is not set in any environment (including production during a misconfigured deployment), all sessions are signed with the publicly known string 'dev-secret-change-in-production'. An attacker knowing this value can forge valid session cookies.
owasp_findings.remediation: Remove the fallback string. Instead, throw an error at startup if SESSION_SECRET is not set or is shorter than a minimum length (e.g., 32 bytes): 'if (!process.env.SESSION_SECRET || process.env.SESSION_SECRET.length < 32) throw new Error("SESSION_SECRET environment variable is required and must be at least 32 characters");'

owasp_findings.title: Missing HSTS header on both vhosts; missing CSP on public vhost; unsafe-inline in admin CSP
owasp_findings.description: Neither the public nor the admin nginx vhost sets a Strict-Transport-Security header, so browsers will not enforce HTTPS on subsequent visits. The public vhost (lines 75-78) has no Content-Security-Policy, leaving public visitors fully exposed to any XSS payload. The admin vhost CSP (line 132) includes 'unsafe-inline' for both script-src and style-src, which defeats XSS mitigation for admin users even though the CSP header is present.
owasp_findings.remediation: Add 'add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload" always;' to both server blocks. Add a Content-Security-Policy to the public vhost. Remove 'unsafe-inline' from the admin CSP's script-src; instead, use nonces or hashes for the one inline script in the admin tag list page. Load Bootstrap and SortableJS from a self-hosted copy or use SRI hashes on the CDN links.

owasp_findings.title: Redis session store has no authentication configured
owasp_findings.description: The Redis service starts with only '--appendonly yes'. There is no password (requirepass) configured. Any process reachable on the Docker internal network can read, write, or delete session keys directly without authentication. A compromised container on the internal network can enumerate all sessions and hijack any admin session.
owasp_findings.remediation: Add a Redis password via the command flag '--requirepass <strong-password>' and pass the same password in the REDIS_URL environment variable: 'redis://:password@redis:6379'. Store the password as a Docker secret or environment variable sourced from a secrets manager.

owasp_findings.category: A01:2021 - Broken Access Control (Missing SameSite Cookie)
owasp_findings.title: Session cookie missing SameSite attribute
owasp_findings.description: The express-session cookie configuration does not set the 'sameSite' attribute. Modern browsers default to 'Lax' for cookies without SameSite, which provides some protection for top-level navigation but does not protect AJAX/fetch-based CSRF. Explicitly setting SameSite is required to ensure consistent behavior across browsers and to establish a deliberate defense-in-depth layer alongside CSRF tokens.
owasp_findings.remediation: Add 'sameSite: "strict"' to the cookie configuration in src/server.ts. Strict is appropriate because the admin portal does not need to be accessed via cross-site links, and it would prevent session cookies from being sent in any cross-site context.

owasp_findings.title: EC2 instance ID hardcoded in public CI/CD workflow
owasp_findings.description: The production EC2 instance ID 'i-021ca6be74a5d9b16' is hardcoded in the workflow file, which is committed to the repository. While not a credential, it discloses infrastructure topology to anyone with repository read access and reduces the reconnaissance effort required for targeted attacks against the AWS account.
owasp_findings.remediation: Move the instance ID to a GitHub Actions environment variable or secret (e.g., secrets.EC2_INSTANCE_ID) and reference it as '${{ secrets.EC2_INSTANCE_ID }}' in the workflow.

owasp_findings.category: A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
owasp_findings.title: Unstructured error logging may emit full stack traces to container stdout
owasp_findings.description: The global error handler logs the full Error object with 'console.error("Unhandled error:", err)', which includes the stack trace. In production this goes to container stdout/stderr visible via SSM session or any log aggregation that lacks access controls. Stack traces disclose internal file paths, module names, and call depth.
owasp_findings.remediation: In production (NODE_ENV === 'production'), log only the error message and a correlation ID, not the full stack. In development, the full stack is acceptable. Use a structured logging library (pino, winston) to control log levels and ensure stack traces are only emitted when the log level permits it.

spoofing.finding: The authentication model is sound: bcrypt with cost 10 for password hashing, Redis-backed sessions with httpOnly cookies, and a requireAuth middleware guarding all protected routes. The primary spoofing risk is the hardcoded default password ('changeme123') in the seed migration (migrations/1706457600001_seed-admin-user.js:13) — if not rotated, an attacker can authenticate as admin with a known credential. A secondary risk is the absent SameSite attribute on the session cookie, which can be combined with CSRF to perform actions as an authenticated user. No token-based auth bypass vectors were identified in the session lookup logic.
controls_present: bcrypt password hashing (cost 10)
httpOnly session cookie
Redis-backed session store
requireAuth middleware on all protected routes
gaps: Default password documented in version control
Session cookie missing SameSite attribute
No account lockout after failed login attempts

tampering.finding: All database queries use parameterized statements — no SQL injection surface was found in any model file. However, the admin JSON API (src/routes/admin.ts:407, 412) passes req.body directly to model methods, creating a mass-assignment surface. The tag drag-drop PATCH endpoint (line 441) similarly passes req.body to TagModel.update() without field restriction. Data integrity in transit is ensured by TLS (nginx handles SSL termination with TLSv1.2/1.3 only, strong cipher suites). The CSRF gap means any form submission can be tampered with cross-origin.
controls_present: Parameterized SQL throughout all model files
TLS 1.2/1.3 with strong ciphers on nginx
Input validation on tag value field (regex [a-z0-9-]+)
Transaction wrapping for multi-step place/tag operations
gaps: Mass assignment via admin JSON API (req.body passed directly to model)
No CSRF tokens — cross-origin form tampering possible
URL field not validated for safe protocol before storage

repudiation.finding: Nginx logs all requests with the standard 'main' format including IP, timestamp, URI, status, and user agent (nginx/nginx.conf:16-18). No application-level audit log exists for admin actions (create, update, delete). An admin action cannot be tied to a specific user ID in the logs — only the IP and session are traceable. There are no mechanisms to prevent an admin from denying that they performed a destructive action.
controls_present: Nginx access logging enabled
Timestamps on all database records (created_at, updated_at)
Session-based identity available at request time
gaps: No application-level audit trail for admin CRUD operations
Admin user identity not logged alongside mutations
No immutable log storage (logs go to container stdout, which is ephemeral without a log aggregator)

information_disclosure.finding: The public API (/api/places, /api/tags) is intentionally unauthenticated and returns only public business directory data — no user data, no session data, no internal IDs beyond UUIDs. The /admin/api/me endpoint (src/routes/admin.ts:388) returns the full req.user object (id, email, name, timestamps) without the password hash, which is acceptable. The global error handler (src/server.ts:92-95) responds to clients with only 'Internal server error' — no stack trace leaks to HTTP responses. The primary disclosure risk is the EC2 instance ID in the public workflow file and the default password hash in the migration.
controls_present: Error handler returns generic message to client
password_hash field stripped before placing user in session
Public API returns only necessary fields
Separate public and admin routing
gaps: EC2 instance ID hardcoded in public CI/CD workflow
Default password hash committed to version control
Full stack traces logged to container stdout in production
No CSP on public site (facilitates data exfiltration if XSS is exploited)

denial_of_service.finding: Nginx applies rate limiting to the public API (10r/s, burst 20) and the login endpoint (5r/m, burst 5). No rate limiting is applied to authenticated admin CRUD endpoints or the admin JSON API. The admin tag PATCH endpoint is called once per row on every drag-drop reorder event (src/views/admin/tags/index.ejs:216-219), firing N parallel requests where N is the total number of tags — this is a minor amplification path for a logged-in attacker. The database connection pool is bounded at 20 connections (src/db.ts:6). The process.exit(-1) call on pool error (src/db.ts:13-15) is a self-DoS vector if the database becomes transiently unavailable.
controls_present: Nginx rate limiting on public API and login endpoint
DB connection pool bounded at max 20
Redis session store prevents session memory exhaustion in Node
gaps: No rate limiting on admin CRUD/API endpoints
process.exit(-1) on idle pool error causes self-DoS on transient DB issues
No request body size limit configured explicitly (Express defaults apply)

elevation_of_privilege.finding: The application runs as a non-root user inside the container (Dockerfile:26-49, user 'nodejs' uid 1001). The Docker network isolates app, postgres, and redis on an internal bridge with only nginx exposed externally. The admin subdomain routing is done at the application layer based on hostname (src/server.ts:58-68), not at nginx. This means any request to the main site at /admin/* will reach admin routes if the Host header can be manipulated — however, requireAuth is applied via router.use(requireAuth) at line 60 of admin.ts, so unauthenticated requests are redirected to login regardless of Host routing. No privilege escalation path from public user to admin was found beyond the CSRF chain. The admin API has no role hierarchy — all authenticated users are equivalent admins.
controls_present: Container runs as non-root (nodejs uid 1001)
requireAuth middleware applied via router.use() covering all routes below it
Docker internal network isolates postgres and redis
Admin actions require active session
gaps: No IP allowlisting on admin subdomain (any IP can attempt admin login)
Subdomain routing logic in application layer rather than nginx — relies on Host header
No multi-factor authentication on admin login
CSRF gap allows privilege actions if admin session is active and admin visits attacker page

findings.description: Bcrypt hash of the documented default admin password 'changeme123' hardcoded in version-controlled migration file
findings.remediation: Remove the DEFAULT_PASSWORD_HASH constant and the plaintext password from the comment. Generate a secure random password at deployment time and inject it via a secrets manager rather than committing any credential or its hash to source control.

findings.description: Hardcoded session secret fallback string 'dev-secret-change-in-production' in application startup code
findings.remediation: Remove the fallback string. Require SESSION_SECRET to be explicitly set in the environment and throw a startup error if it is absent or too short.
This is an example file and the credentials are for local development only — acceptable. Ensure .env is in .gitignore (confirmed present) and that production values are never placed in .env.example.
